topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the dull thud of something heavy hitting a desk. Subject line: Party Update. My thumb hovered over the 'delete' button, then hovered again, a brief, pointless flicker of hope dying instantly. I read the text twice. 'Due to unforeseen circumstances, the surprise celebration for Sarah has been cancelled.' A slow, heavy sigh escaped my lips, the air in the room suddenly feeling thick and unmoving. I leaned back in my chair, the worn leather groaning under my weight. There was no anger, just a strange flatness settling over my chest. I picked up a pen, the plastic cool against my fingertips, and stared blankly at the blank page. The anticipation that had been humming beneath my skin for weeks simply evaporated, leaving behind a quiet, empty space.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, my breath catching in my throat before I could force it out. The words swam before my eyes: cancellation. My carefully constructed internal plan, the weeks of secret coordination, all reduced to this single, impersonal notification. I didn't move. My hands, resting on the keyboard, felt strangely numb, heavy as stones. I let my gaze drift to the window, watching the rain streak down the glass, each drop a tiny, monotonous reminder of things slipping through my grasp. A small, almost imperceptible tremor ran through my jaw. I closed my eyes for a long moment, letting the silence of the apartment swallow the sudden, hollow feeling in my stomach. It was just… over.

[story 3]

When Mark finally spoke the news, his voice was surprisingly even, stripped of the usual buoyant excitement. "So, the party's off." I didn't respond immediately. I simply nodded, a small, jerky motion. I watched the confetti I’d mentally pictured—the streamers, the laughter, the collective gasp—dissolve into the sterile reality of the notification on my phone. My shoulders slumped, a noticeable sinking into my seat. I picked up my coffee mug, the ceramic warm against my palms, but I didn't drink. The liquid remained untouched, mirroring the lack of energy that had flooded my system. I set the mug down with deliberate slowness, the small sound echoing loudly in the quiet room.
